questions.stem: A disease-causing organism is known as a:
questions.explanation: A pathogen is a disease-causing organism, typically a virus, bacterium, fungus or protist.

questions.stem: Which domain is NOT known to cause diseases in humans?
questions.explanation: Archaea are not known to cause any diseases in humans, unlike viruses, bacteria, fungi and protists.

questions.stem: The skin acts as a primary defence by being:

options.text: Both a physical and chemical barrier

questions.explanation: The skin (and mucous membranes) act as both physical and chemical barriers to pathogens.

questions.scenario: A cut in the skin is sealed by a blood clot.
questions.stem: The clot forms when fibrinogen is converted to:
questions.explanation: Clotting factors from platteets trigger a cascade converting fibrinogen to fibrin by thrombin, trapping erythrocytes.

questions.stem: Which cell fragments release clotting factors to start blood clotting?
questions.explanation: Platelets release clotting factors that begin the cascade sealing a cut in the skin.

questions.stem: The innate immune system:

options.text: Responds to broad categories of pathogen and does not change during life
Builds up a specific memory of each pathogen

questions.explanation: The innate system responds to broad categories of pathogen and does not change during the organism's life.

questions.stem: The adaptive immune system differs from the innate system because it:

options.text: Responds specifically and builds a memory of pathogens

questions.explanation: The adaptive system responds specifically to particular pathogens and builds a memory, becoming more effective over time.

questions.scenario: A phagocyte engulfs a pathogen.
questions.stem: It engulfs the pathogen by:
questions.explanation: Phagocytes recognize pathogens, engulf them by endocytosis and digest them with enzymes from lysosomes.

questions.stem: Phagocytes digest engulfed pathogens using enzymes from:
questions.explanation: After engulfing pathogens, phagocytes digest them using enzymes from lysosomes.

questions.stem: Which cells of the adaptive immune system produce antibodies?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes cooperate to produce antibodies; each B-lymphocyte makes a specific type of antibody.

questions.stem: An antigen is a molecule that:
questions.explanation: Antigens are recognition molecules, usually surface glycoproteins or proteins, that trigger antibody production.

questions.scenario: Blood is transfused into a person with a different blood group.
questions.stem: Antigens on the donor erythrocytes may:

options.text: Stimulate antibody production in the recipient

questions.explanation: Antigens on erythrocytes can stimulate antibody production if transfused into someone with a different blood group.

questions.scenario: A B-lymphocyte becomes activated.
questions.stem: Activation requires the antigen and contact with an activated:
questions.explanation: B-cells are activated by direct interaction with the specific antigen and contact with a helper T-cell activated by the same antigen.

questions.scenario: An activated B-lymphocyte divides to produce many antibody-secreting cells.
questions.stem: These antibody-secreting cells are called:
questions.explanation: Activated B-cells divide by mitosis to form clones of plasma cells that secrete large quantities of antibody.

questions.stem: Why must activated B-cells divide by mitosis?

options.text: To produce enough plasma cells to make sufficient antibody

questions.explanation: Few B-cells respond to a given antigen, so they divide to make large numbers of antibody-producing plasma cells.

questions.stem: Immunity is due to the long-term survival of:
questions.explanation: Immunity results from retaining memory cells that can quickly make the specific antibodies needed.

questions.stem: Immunity is best defined as the ability to:

options.text: Eliminate an infectious disease from the body

questions.explanation: Immunity is the ability to eliminate an infectious disease, due to long-lived memory cells.

questions.stem: HIV is transmitted through:
questions.explanation: HIV is transmitted in body fluids by various mechanisms.

questions.scenario: HIV infects and kills certain lymphocytes.
questions.stem: The consequence of this is:

options.text: A reduced ability to produce antibodies and fight infections

questions.explanation: By killing certain lymphocytes, HIV reduces antibody production and the ability to fight opportunistic infections, causing AIDS.

questions.stem: Antibiotics work by blocking processes that occur in:
questions.explanation: Antibiotics block processes specific to bacteria, not eukaryotic cells, so they harm bacteria but not the host.

questions.stem: Why do antibiotics fail to control viral infections?

options.text: Viruses lack the bacterial processes antibiotics target

questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial processes; viruses do not carry these out, so antibiotics do not affect them.

questions.scenario: Some bacteria have evolved resistance to several antibiotics.
questions.stem: To slow the emergence of multiresistant bacteria, it is necessary to:

options.text: Use antibiotics as much as possible

questions.explanation: Careful use of antibiotics is necessary to slow the evolution of multiresistant bacteria.

questions.scenario: A disease can transfer from another animal species to humans.
questions.stem: Such a disease is called a:
questions.explanation: Zoonoses are infectious diseases that can transfer from other species to humans, such as rabies and COVID-19.

questions.stem: Which is given as an example of a zoonosis?
questions.explanation: Zoonoses include tuberculosis, rabies, Japanese encephalitis and COVID-19.

questions.stem: Vaccines stimulate immunity by containing:

options.text: Antigens, or nucleic acids coding for antigens

questions.explanation: Vaccines contain antigens (or nucleic acids coding for antigens) to develop immunity without causing the disease.

questions.stem: A vaccine develops immunity to a pathogen:

options.text: Only by causing the full disease

questions.explanation: Vaccines stimulate immunity to a specific pathogen without causing the disease itself.

questions.scenario: A high percentage of a population is immune to a disease.
questions.stem: This provides:
questions.explanation: Herd immunity arises when enough of a population is immune that transmission is greatly impeded, protecting others too.

questions.stem: Herd immunity shows that members of a population are:
questions.explanation: Herd immunity depends on interdependence: widespread immunity impedes transmission and protects the population.

questions.scenario: Cases of a disease rise from 200 to 250.
questions.stem: The percentage change is:
questions.explanation: Percentage change = (250 − 200) ÷ 200 × 100 = 25%.

questions.stem: Which of these is typically classed as a pathogen?
questions.explanation: Pathogens are disease-causing organisms such as viruses, bacteria, fungi and protists.

questions.scenario: Phagocytes move from the blood to a site of infection.
questions.stem: This movement is described as:
questions.explanation: Phagocytes use amoeboid movement to travel from the blood to sites of infection.

questions.stem: Lymphocytes are found circulating in the blood and contained in:
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes circulate in the blood and are contained in lymph nodes.

questions.stem: Antigens are usually located:

options.text: On the outer surfaces of pathogens

questions.explanation: Antigens, mostly glycoproteins or proteins, are usually on the outer surfaces of pathogens.

questions.stem: A B-cell becomes a memory cell or plasma cell only after it has been:
questions.explanation: B-cells produce antibodies and become memory cells only after activation by antigen and a helper T-cell.

questions.scenario: A person who recovered from a disease resists it if exposed again.
questions.stem: This is due to:

options.text: Memory cells producing a faster, stronger response

questions.explanation: Long-lived memory cells enable a faster, stronger immune response on re-exposure, giving immunity.

questions.stem: AIDS develops as a consequence of HIV infecting and killing:
questions.explanation: HIV infects and kills certain lymphocytes, weakening the immune response and leading to AIDS.

questions.scenario: A new technique searches chemical libraries for antibiotics.
questions.stem: According to NOS, this shows that new techniques can:
questions.explanation: The development of new techniques, such as searching chemical libraries, can open new avenues of research yielding new antibiotics.

questions.scenario: COVID-19 recently transferred to humans from another species.
questions.stem: This makes COVID-19 an example of a:
questions.explanation: COVID-19 is a zoonosis, recently transferred from another species with profound consequences for humans.

questions.scenario: Some modern vaccines contain mRNA rather than whole antigens.
questions.stem: The mRNA works by:

options.text: Coding for antigens so the body makes them and develops immunity

questions.explanation: Vaccines can contain nucleic acids (DNA or RNA) coding for antigens, so the body makes the antigen and develops immunity.

questions.scenario: Region A reports 40 cases and Region B reports 50 cases.
questions.stem: The percentage difference relative to Region A is:
questions.explanation: Percentage difference = (50 − 40) ÷ 40 × 100 = 25%.

questions.stem: Which cell type is the only component of the innate immune system students must know?
questions.explanation: Students are only required to know phagocytes as a component of the innate immune system.

questions.stem: Besides the skin, which structures act as a primary defence?
questions.explanation: Skin and mucous membranes together form the primary defence against pathogens.

questions.scenario: During clotting, erythrocytes are trapped in a mesh.
questions.stem: The mesh is formed from:
questions.explanation: Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin, which forms a mesh trapping erythrocytes to make a clot.

questions.stem: Each B-lymphocyte makes:
questions.explanation: An individual has a large number of B-lymphocytes, each making a specific type of antibody.

questions.stem: Why don't antibiotics harm human cells?

options.text: They target processes found in bacteria but not eukaryotic cells

questions.explanation: Antibiotics block bacterial processes that eukaryotic (human) cells do not carry out, so the host is unharmed.

questions.scenario: Careful 19th-century observations during epidemics led to disease-control breakthroughs.
questions.stem: According to NOS, this shows that:

options.text: Careful observation can lead to important progress

questions.explanation: Careful observation, as during 19th-century epidemics of childbed fever and cholera, can lead to important progress.

questions.stem: HIV cannot easily be transmitted through:

options.text: Casual contact such as shaking hands

questions.explanation: HIV is transmitted in body fluids, not by casual contact such as shaking hands.

questions.scenario: Vaccination rates fall below the level needed for herd immunity.
questions.stem: The likely consequence is:
questions.explanation: If too few are immune, herd immunity is lost and transmission becomes easier, risking epidemics.

questions.scenario: Vaccines are tested rigorously before use.
questions.stem: According to NOS, consumers should be aware that:

options.text: Media may report on research while evaluation is still ongoing
Side effects are always guaranteed to be severe

questions.explanation: Scientists publish research for evaluation; media may report before evaluation is complete, so consumers should be aware.

questions.stem: A clone of plasma cells produced from one activated B-cell all make:
questions.explanation: Plasma cells cloned from one activated B-cell all produce the same specific antibody, in large amounts.
